title: SEGMENT 3 — Norman Pace 1985 and the birth of culture-independent microbiology

lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — Norman Pace 1985 and the birth of culture-independent microbiology
The year is 1985.
Kary Mullis has just invented the polymerase chain reaction — PCR, the technique that lets you amplify any specific stretch of DNA exponentially, starting from vanishingly small amounts.

Norman Pace, a microbiologist who had been working on RNA biochemistry at the University of Colorado at Denver, is sitting in his office thinking about Carl Woese's work on 16S rRNA, and about all the bacteria in the world that he can't culture, and about what PCR might let him do.
The idea that hit him is, in retrospect, beautiful in its simplicity.
You don't have to culture a microbe to read its 16S gene.

You can extract DNA directly from an environmental sample — a scoop of soil, a milliliter of seawater, a fecal swab — design PCR primers that match the conserved regions of the 16S gene, and amplify the variable regions in between.
The conserved regions are conserved in everything, so the primers will grab the 16S from every bacterium and archaeon in the sample.
The variable regions are variable, so the amplified products will carry the phylogenetic signal that tells you who's there.
No culturing required.
The unculturable majority becomes visible for the first time.
Pace and his collaborators published the foundational papers between 1985 and 1990.

They went to a hot spring in Yellowstone National Park, took a sample of microbial mat, extracted total DNA, PCR-amplified the 16S genes, cloned them into bacterial vectors, and sequenced the clones one by one.
What they found was astonishing.
Most of the 16S sequences they recovered did not match anything in the existing databases of cultured organisms.
They were entirely new lineages of bacteria and archaea — sometimes whole new phyla — that nobody had ever seen, because nobody had ever managed to grow them.
The hot spring contained orders of magnitude more microbial diversity than had been suspected.

And this pattern repeated everywhere Pace's group looked: the open ocean, the deep subsurface, hydrothermal vents, freshwater lakes, every environment they sampled was teeming with previously invisible microbial lineages.
The implication was profound.
Microbiology, as it had been practiced for a century, had been studying a tiny, biased slice of microbial life — the slice that happened to grow on standard lab media.
Everything else, the vast majority, had been invisible.
Pace's culture-independent approach exposed a hidden diversity that overturned essentially every estimate of how many kinds of microbes existed and what they were doing.
The number of recognized bacterial phyla, which had been a few dozen for most of the twentieth century, exploded into the hundreds.
Many of these new phyla had no cultured representatives at all — we knew they existed only because their 16S sequences kept turning up in environmental samples.
We called these the candidate phyla, organisms that we had identified phylogenetically but had never seen alive in the lab.
As of the mid-2020s, there are probably more candidate phyla than cultured ones.
The known microbial world is a small island in a vast unknown sea, and the sea was first mapped by Pace and his successors using exactly this 16S sequencing trick.
Let me make the workflow concrete, because the basic recipe Pace established is, with some refinements, still how 16S microbiome surveys are done today.
You start with a sample — let's say a stool sample, since that's where most of you will encounter this.
Step one: DNA extraction.
You lyse all the cells in the sample, releasing their DNA, and you purify the DNA away from proteins, lipids, and PCR inhibitors.

This is harder than it sounds, because different bacteria have different cell wall structures — gram-positive bacteria with their thick peptidoglycan walls are much harder to break open than gram-negative bacteria — and any extraction method will be biased.
If you don't lyse the tough cells efficiently, you'll under-represent them in your final dataset.
Modern kits use a combination of mechanical bead-beating, enzymatic digestion, and chemical lysis to try to break everything open uniformly.
The choice of extraction kit is one of the major sources of variability between microbiome studies, and people argue about it endlessly.
Step two: PCR amplification of a 16S variable region.

You design primers that anneal to two conserved regions flanking one or two of the variable regions — most often the V3-V4 region, sometimes just V4, sometimes V1-V2, depending on the platform and the question.
The primers have the conserved-region sequences at their 3' ends, so they bind to essentially every bacterial and archaeal 16S gene in the sample.
At the 5' ends, the primers carry extra sequences that will let you load the products onto an Illumina sequencer — adapter sequences, sample-specific barcodes, and so on.

You run PCR for, typically, 25 to 30 cycles, and you end up with a tube full of millions of copies of the V3-V4 region from every microbe in your original sample, each copy tagged with a sample barcode.
Step three: sequencing.
You pool the PCR products from many samples together — multiplexing — and load them onto an Illumina MiSeq or NextSeq instrument, which reads the variable region from both ends in paired-end mode.
Modern protocols target the V4 region with 250-base paired-end reads, which fully overlaps the 250-base V4 amplicon and gives you very accurate sequences.
The MiSeq, in particular, became the workhorse of 16S microbiome surveys because its read length and error profile matched the V3-V4 amplicon almost perfectly.
A single MiSeq run can sequence hundreds of samples at tens of thousands of reads each.
Step four: clustering and taxonomy.
You take all the millions of reads, you bin them by sample using the barcodes, and then you have to decide which reads come from which organism.
This is where there's historically been a big methodological debate, and it leads to the OTU-versus-ASV distinction that I want to spend a moment on, because it's important.
The older approach is to cluster reads into OTUs, operational taxonomic units.
You take all the reads, you compute pairwise similarities, and you lump together any reads that are more than 97% identical into a single OTU.
The 97% threshold is a rough approximation of the species boundary — two 16S sequences that are 97% identical are usually from organisms classified as the same species.
Each OTU is then treated as a single bacterial "species" for downstream analysis.
The advantage of OTUs is that they're robust to sequencing errors — if a read has a few errors in it, it still falls into the same OTU as the correct version.
The disadvantage is that you lose resolution.

Two strains of the same species might have 16S sequences that differ by only one or two nucleotides, well under the 97% threshold, and they'd be lumped together as a single OTU even though they might be biologically very different.
The newer approach, which has largely won, is to call ASVs, amplicon sequence variants.

Instead of clustering, you use a denoising algorithm — the most famous is DADA2, developed by Ben Callahan and Susan Holmes around 2016 — that statistically models the sequencing error process and tries to recover the exact original sequences that gave rise to the observed reads.
The output is a set of unique sequences, often differing by just a single nucleotide, each treated as a separate biological entity.

ASVs give you much higher resolution than OTUs and they're reproducible across studies, which OTUs weren't — if I cluster your reads at 97% in my lab and you cluster them in yours, we'll get different OTU definitions.
ASVs are defined by exact sequence, so they're the same in every dataset that contains them.
The analogy.
OTU is like grouping people by family name — anyone named Smith goes in the Smith bucket, regardless of which specific Smith they are.
ASV is like grouping people by their full name and birthdate — each individual is its own entry.
OTU loses resolution but is forgiving of typos in the data.
ASV preserves resolution and is unforgiving but more accurate when the underlying methods can handle it.
Modern 16S analysis essentially always uses ASVs now, and the standard pipeline is DADA2 followed by taxonomy assignment.
Step five: taxonomy assignment.
You compare each ASV's sequence to a reference database of 16S sequences with known taxonomic labels.
The major reference databases are SILVA, GreenGenes, and the RDP — Ribosomal Database Project.
The algorithm finds the closest matches in the database and assigns a taxonomic label to your ASV: kingdom, phylum, class, order, family, genus, and sometimes species.

You end up with a table where rows are ASVs, columns are samples, and each cell contains a count of how many reads of that ASV were found in that sample, plus a separate taxonomy table mapping each ASV to a taxonomic lineage.
That table — the feature table — is the core data object of a 16S study.
Every downstream analysis, every diversity calculation, every comparison between groups, starts from that table.
The whole pipeline — DNA extraction, PCR, sequencing, denoising, taxonomy assignment — has been packaged into a few standard software toolkits.
QIIME2 is the dominant one; Mothur is the older alternative; DADA2 as an R package is what most analysts actually use for the denoising step inside QIIME2.
If you read a microbiome methods section, you'll almost always see one of those three named.
They are the workhorses.
That's the 16S workflow.

It's now twenty years old in its essential form, refined enormously in the details, but the bones are still Norman Pace's 1985 idea: pull DNA from the sample, amplify the 16S, read the sequences, identify who's there.
Let's move on to what 16S can't tell you, and the technology that fills the gap.
